title: Beyond semitoric
speaker: Susan Tolman
abstract: A compact four dimensional completely integrable system f:M→R2 is semitoric if it has only non-degenerate singularities, without hyperbolic blocks, and one of the components of generates a circle action. Semitoric systems have been extensively studied and have many nice properties: for example, the preimages f−1(x) are all connected. Unfortunately, although there are many interesting examples of semitoric systems, the class has some limitation. For example, there are blowups of S2×S2 with Hamiltonian circle actions which cannot be extended to semitoric systems.

proof_stages.label: Integrable and semitoric baseline
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes the definition of an integrable system, recalls the standard local singularity types used in semitoric systems, and reviews semitoric connectedness, classification, minimal models, and quantization as the template to be generalized.

proof_stages.label: Four-dimensional circle-action test cases
proof_stages.summary: Concrete Hamiltonian S^1-actions on blow-ups of S^2 x S^2 are used to show that semitoric systems extend beyond toric systems but still exclude natural circle actions, motivating a broader framework.

proof_stages.label: Complexity-one local model and defining polynomial
proof_stages.summary: The higher-dimensional replacement for semitoric geometry is set up as a complexity-one T^{n-1}-space, whose local normal form and tall reduced spaces admit a defining polynomial identifying the local quotient with C.

proof_stages.label: Ephemeral critical points and near-toric systems
proof_stages.summary: Using the defining polynomial and Taylor data on the reduced space, the speaker defines ephemeral critical points, observes that focus-focus and related resonance models fit the definition, and states the near-toric connectedness theorem.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to Morse theory for connected fibers
proof_stages.summary: For a fixed moment value, the proof reduces to a surface on which the induced function can be made Morse, ephemeral points become regular, toric-type points become extrema, no index-one critical points occur, and hence the original fibers are connected.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and obstructions from the Morse picture
proof_stages.summary: The same Morse-theoretic analysis implies that reduced spaces are S^2 and gives quick proofs that the earlier blow-up example is not toric and that the further blow-up example is not semitoric, using critical-point counts and fixed-point weights.

proof_stages.label: Robustness of the near-toric condition
proof_stages.summary: The speaker notes that nondegenerate complexity-one systems without hyperbolic blocks are near-toric, that the ephemeral condition has a coordinate-free jet formulation, and that the main theorem only needs properness of the moment map rather than compactness.

proof_stages.label: Open program and related generalizations
proof_stages.summary: The final part proposes constructing near-toric structures on genus-zero complexity-one spaces, discusses classification and toric-extension questions via the painting invariant, and situates the work among physical resonances, hypersemitoric systems, almost-toric fibrations, cohomology, and monodromy.

examples.name: Toric action on S^2 x S^2
examples.note: The product sphere with rotation of the first factor illustrates a Hamiltonian circle action that is secretly the standard toric T^2-action.
examples.engagement_reason: She describes the S^2 x S^2 circle moment map, identifies its two fixed spheres and their min/max moment values, and notes the concrete extension to the standard toric S^1^2 action.

examples.name: Three equal blow-ups giving a semitoric non-toric action
examples.note: Blowing up three equal-size points on one fixed sphere in S^2 x S^2 supplies a concrete semitoric system that fails to extend to a toric action, later rederived from the Morse-theoretic count of critical points.
examples.engagement_reason: She constructs the three equal blow-ups on a fixed sphere, counts the resulting fixed spheres and fixed points, tracks their common moment value, and uses this setup to distinguish semitoric from toric.

passes.reason: The equal three-blow-up construction is a systematic semitoric-but-not-toric example refuting toric extendability.

examples.name: Further blow-up giving a nonsemitoric circle action

examples.engagement_reason: She constructs the further blow-up, counts the fixed spheres, six fixed points, and Z_2-fixed spheres, and later checks the fixed-point weights to explain why it cannot be semitoric.

examples.name: Fractional resonance C^2 model
examples.note: The C^2 action with weights p and -q and invariant x^q y^p makes the defining polynomial and the ephemeral condition concrete, and is later mentioned as a physical source of examples to test globally.
examples.engagement_reason: She writes the weighted C^2 action, computes the moment map and defining polynomial x^q y^p, explicitly checks its invariance, and then uses its imaginary part as an ephemeral example.

passes.reason: The fractional p:q resonance has higher-order invariant x^q y^p, giving a degenerate ephemeral local singularity.

examples.engagement_reason: She gives the C^3 action, writes its moment map components, identifies xyz as the defining invariant polynomial, and uses Im(xyz) in the ephemeral construction.

examples.name: Z_2-stabilizer model with defining polynomial z^2
examples.note: The local model S^1 x_{Z_2}(R x C) shows how a disconnected stabilizer yields the invariant z^2 and explains why some hyperbolic-looking points are allowed as ephemeral.
examples.engagement_reason: She spells out the S^1 ×_{Z_2} R × C local model, its circle action, moment map, defining polynomial z^2, and later relates it to allowed hyperbolic behavior.

passes.reason: The standard quadratic Z_2-stabilizer model is cited to refute that hyperbolic points can never be ephemeral.

examples.name: Positive-genus reduced torus obstruction
examples.note: The torus is used as the motivating obstruction showing why higher-genus reduced surfaces cannot support the desired connected-fiber picture, forcing the genus-zero focus of the open question.
examples.engagement_reason: She reasons through the positive-genus obstruction by explaining that a torus or higher-genus reduced surface cannot support the required connected-fiber Morse-type function.
